Lithium-ion batteries: outlook on present, future, and hybridized technologies

Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) continue to draw vast attention as a promising energy storage technology due to their high energy density, low self-discharge property, nearly zero-memory effect, high open circuit voltage, and long lifespan. In particular, high-energy density lithium-ion batteries are considered as the ideal power source for electric vehicles (EVs) and hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs) in the automotive industry, in recent years. This review discusses key aspects of the present and the future battery technologies on the basis of the working electrode. We then discuss how lithium-ion batteries evolve to meet the growing demand on high charge capacity and electrode stability. An account of a stand-alone energy device (off-grid system) that combines an energy harvesting technology with a lithium-ion battery is also provided. The main discussion is categorized into three perspectives such as the evolution from the conventional to the advanced LIBs (e.g., Li-rich transition metal oxide and Ni-rich transition metal oxide batteries), to the state-of-the-art LIBs (e.g., Li-air, Li-sulfur batteries, organic electrode batteries, solid-state batteries, and
batteries), and to the hybridized LIBs (e.g., metal halide perovskite batteries).

Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are extensively employed as power sources in a wide range of applications owing to their high energy densities, high coulombic efficiencies, low self-discharge features, and a range of chemical potentials accessible with diverse electrode designs.1-4 Especially, the past decade has seen a growing trend towards next-generation lithium-ion batteries with high charge capacities/power densities developed for electric vehicles (EVs), hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs), aerospace applications, and autonomous electric devices (e.g. hybrid solar batteries).2,5-9 However, conventional lithium-ion batteries, which are mainly based on the

crucial issues, which need to be addressed for their successful commercialization, such as phase transition, electrolyte decomposition, formation of an unstable solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI), volume expansion and/or surface reconstruction of active material particles, and electrode degradation during charge and discharge. On the other side, a hybrid device that merges two different energy applications (e.g. a solar-cell and a lithium-ion battery) has drawn much attention in the most recent years. This new technology, often regarded as a stand-alone energy system, provides high energy efficiency with reliable power in a remote location. For instance, a photo-rechargeable perovskite

or Br) battery can both convert and store energy, serving as an off-grid system. However, an alternative cell configuration with a new class of electrode material is required to realize these types of applications.

In this review, we will discuss the key issues of the present and commercialized lithium-ion batteries, on the basis of the working electrodes. A further discussion will be focused on some state-of-the-art battery technologies, comparing their advantages and disadvantages. Also, particular attention is devoted to a hybridized device (off-grid system) that combines an energy harvesting application with the energy storage technology. Taken together, we present the evolution of lithium-ion batteries (working electrodes) from conventional technologies into advanced, state-of-the-art, and hybridized technologies. The current survey sheds some light on understanding the future direction of the next-generation lithium-ion batteries, Fig. 1.

(NMC811), owing to their higher discharge capacities (215–220 mA h g

) and lower cost as compared to LCO and spinel batteries.[56,72-74] The increase of the Ni concentration in the electrode composite significantly contributes to the increment of the charge capacities (Fig. 6a), due to the two-stage oxidation changes between

) required for the charge compensation mechanism of the Ni-rich cathode leads to a severe degradation process.[42,43,75] The aggressive capacity fading is typically connected to the NiO surface reconstruction or clustering on the Ni-rich active material particles (Fig. 6c).[42] This involves not only rapid electrolyte decomposition, but also phase transition from a layered (

) phase into a spinel-like and/or rock-salt phase on charge and discharge.[42,75] To counter the structural mismatch, Sun et al. demonstrated a nanostructured high-energy cathode with a concentration gradient between Ni, Mn, and Co (Fig. 6b).[72]
As a next generation battery technology, sulfur (S) is a promising candidate for cathode materials (Fig. 7c). It offers a very high theoretical capacity of

, about 10 times that of current transition metal oxides. It has higher abundance than cobalt and nickel in the earth's crust, and is of very low cost.[76,77] The schematic of the working principle of a Li-S battery and its galvanostatic profile are shown in Fig. 7a and b. In spite of these significant advantages, a number of technical challenges hinder the further applications of sulfur cathodes. They suffer from low electrochemical potential vs.
, low electrical conductivity, and like other conversion type electrodes, sulfur suffers from a significant volume expansion of

, causing material cracking and detachment from the electrode conductive network.[78,79] Also, the sulfur based cathode is of low vaporization temperature, leading to a pure sulfur loss during the electrode drying process under vacuum.[80] In addition, intermediate reaction products formed during charge and discharge, i.e., polysulfide

), are soluble in the electrolyte, and thus lead to the shuttle mechanism and capacity loss.[81-84] The long-chain polysulfides dissolved in the electrolyte will migrate back to the anode, and they will be reduced into lower order polysulfides. This process is followed by the migration of the lower order polysulfides back to the cathode, resulting in a re-oxidation. The shuttle effect will produce insoluble products such as
, causing self-discharge and overall poor coulombic efficiency. These technical barriers prevent the mass commercialization of sulfur cathodes for Li-S batteries despite decades of research.

Using organic materials as electrodes for lithium-ion batteries has been proposed over the last few decades, but they have drawn less attention than the metal based electrodes.[119,120] This battery type benefits from good sustainability, eco-friendly feature, flexibility in material design, and resource abundance, owing to the

presence of lightweight elements such as C, H, N and O.
Several types of organic materials, i.e., organosulfurs, carbonyl compounds, organic radicals (or radical polymers), and imine groups
have been vastly studied in the past two decades.

In particular, carbonyl based compounds (e.g. quinone) are of great interest because of their high redox activity, high redox potential, and abundance. Their redox activity with reversible electron transfer could be extended to multi-electron reactions when additional carbonyl groups are conjugated, forming multivalent anions.
In 1972, Alt et al. reported the utilization of quinone compounds as a cathode for secondary batteries.
Chloranil was used as an active material for the battery, showing
capacity retention after 50 electrochemical cycles (current rate: 0.5C).
However, batteries with organic electrode materials also have major drawbacks such as low-energy density, poor conductivity, poor reaction kinetics, and high solubility in electrolytes.

Common strategies to overcome these problems are addition of conductive additives (e.g. carbon), modification of the structure with conductive materials (e.g. carbon nanotubes or graphene), and tuning the electrode-level.
Previous studies by Luo et al. have suggested azo
functional groups as alternative organic materials to tackle the aforementioned drawbacks (Fig. 10).
Whereas the carbonyl groups or imine groups require more than two functional groups, the azo based electrode only needs one functional group, which in turn simplifies the chemical structure.
The batteries with azo compounds exhibit good cycling stability, as well as high rate capability.

Employing self-assembled organic nanowires using croconic acid disodium salt (CADs) is another notable approach that can deal with the inherent problems of the organic electrodes for lithium-ion batteries.

Solid electrolytes are the key component that enables the development of solid-state batteries. They can be divided into three main categories such as inorganic solid-electrolytes, solid polymer electrolytes, and thin-film solid-electrolytes.

Improvement of conductivity in the solid-electrolytes was therefore a key issue for a long time, and it still remains unclear whether crystalline or amorphous structures can enhance the ion conductivity. Using polymers as solid-electrolytes has the common advantages of good flexibility, high stability with Li-metal, and low shear modulus, but they suffer from low thermal stability, low ionic conductivity, and low oxidation chemical potential. These issues should be challenged for the practical use of the solid polymer electrolytes (SPEs). Thin-film solid electrolytes have also drawn attention as they lead to the development of thin-film batteries that have higher energy density, higher flexibility in application design, and higher compactness as compared to the conventional lithium-ion batteries. Batteries are energy limited and require recharging. On the other hand, batteries can be used to address the intermittency concern of solar cells. Development of photovoltaic-battery integrated systems with high energy density, efficiency, stability, and low-cost is a topic of importance for uninterruptible utilization of electricity generated from solar energy.
Great efforts are being made in this direction with several proposed innovative designs.

; (ii) advanced design that involves the integration of in situ battery storage in solar modules and therefore, offering compactness and fewer packaging requirements with the potential of low-cost (Fig. 15c).

The evolution of lithium-ion batteries strongly relies on the development of working electrodes. Notwithstanding that the current review covers a limited number of electrode types, the surveys certainly add to the comprehensive outlook on the advances of the battery technologies. In the early period, different classes of electrode materials were proposed such as olivine, spinel, and layered materials. This classification is based on the type of

(ionic) diffusion pathways rather than the chemical composition. Since the successful commercialization of LCO, developing batteries with a balance between discharge capacity, thermal stability, capacity retention, and lifespan has become a central issue of battery research. This leads to the development of mixed transition metal oxide cathodes (e.g. NMC), which can provide synergetic benefits over a single transition metal cathode. However, growing demands for lithium-ion batteries to operate with higher discharge capacities and longer cycle numbers have led to a renewed interest in tailoring the existing NMC electrode. The development of Li-rich (or excess) layered oxide cathodes, often denoted as
, Ni, Co), has drawn much attention due to their superior theoretical capacity (

The ever-growing markets of electric vehicles (EVs or HEVs) are now intensively pursuing power sources with higher energy densities. In this regard, Ni-rich layered oxide batteries are likely to be a promising solution to meet the urgent demand, due to their high discharge capacity (

significantly contribute to the charge compensation mechanism, and thus extra capacities can be achieved when the NMC cathode has a higher Ni concentration. Although the high-capacity feature is derived from the conventional

, recent studies have attempted to find the precise and optimum stoichiometric ratio between Ni (high concentration), Mn, and Co to develop a high-performance battery with improved stability and/or good rate capability. We therefore consider the emergence of Li-rich and Ni-rich layered oxides as the initial evolution towards advanced technologies for high energy and power-density lithium-ion batteries. However, there are also critical drawbacks of these battery systems. Li-rich layered oxide batteries undergo an irreversible capacity loss during the initial cycles. At a higher cut-off voltage (

with phase transformations, resulting in dramatic capacity fading during subsequent charge and discharge. Also, Ni-rich layered oxide batteries suffer from electrode degradation by the undesired redox reactions with the electrolyte. Surface reconstruction or clustering by NiO at higher voltage ranges is another major problem which needs to be addressed. Engineering the active material by substituting with other transition metals or employing surface coatings is likely to be helpful in ameliorating the battery performance and stability. Moreover, replacing conventional electrolytes with specially designed electrolytes can be a reasonable treatment for mitigating the aggressive electrolyte decomposition at a high operating voltage. However, some inherent limitations persistently remain, preventing them from being used for high-capacity batteries. Hence, alternative material technology, i.e., conversion electrodes, has been renewed in recent years. This old concept (chemistry) is rising again, leading to the development of new energy storage technologies such as Li-sulfur, Li-air, and

batteries. We regard this as an evolution into the state-of-the-art technologies for lithium-ion batteries. Owing to the conversion mechanism, the discharge capacity and the energy-density could be dramatically enhanced. However, there are also technical barriers that limit the successful commercialization of these applications such as over-potential (Li-air and
), shuttling effect (Li-sulfur), poor rate capability, high interphase resistance (solid-state batteries) and low cycle life.

We are also anticipating the evolution of lithium-ion battery technologies towards self-sufficiency, namely, a stand-alone energy system (or off-grid) for autonomous electronic devices has drawn much attention, recently. The major advantage of using this technology is the high energy efficiency with reliable power at reasonable cost, providing electricity in a remote location. Photo-rechargeable batteries are a good example of the stand-alone energy system, as they combine energy harvesting materials with energy storage materials. A key factor that leads to the successful development of the autonomous device is the seamless integration of the power source (e.g. battery electrode) into the application. Organohalide perovskite (e.g.
) has emerged as a promising photovoltaic material for solar-cells. A problem can arise, however, when an attempt is made to combine the organohalide

perovskite with the battery component without any considerations of the energy level alignment involving the conduction band, valence band, Fermi level, and work function between the electrodes. Successful hybridization with lithium-ion batteries relies on the good compatibility between the active materials.

The present review has shown key evolutions that push the lithium-ion battery technologies forward. Research will continue to push the limits of energy density, power density, rate capability, stability, safety, and cost. On the other hand, the direct incorporation of the energy storage component for the autonomous device would be a new trend of the battery technologies. We hope that this review can offer key insights into the development of the next-generation lithium-ion batteries.
